topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the brutal finality of a dropped stone. My thumb hovered over the screen, a tremor running through my hand before I could even manage to click on the subject line. "Party Cancellation." The words swam before my eyes, blurring the brightly colored photos of everyone I’d spent weeks coordinating with. A slow, cold dread began to pool in my stomach, tightening until it felt like a physical vice. I reread the message, then reread it again, searching for a typo, a mistake. Nothing. Just the stark, undeniable confirmation that the celebration I had meticulously planned—the one I’d poured my heart into—was simply off the table. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and stared blankly at the wall, the silence of the room suddenly deafening.

[story 2]

"Are you sure about this?" I managed to ask, my voice sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. The reply, delivered via text, was curt and devoid of warmth. I stared at the glowing rectangle, the light reflecting sharply off my wide eyes. My jaw felt suddenly slack, my mouth slightly agape. It was impossible. The decorations were bought, the catering confirmed, the venue booked months ago. Now, this. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against the hardwood floor sounding excessively loud in the quiet apartment. I walked over to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to look out at the indifferent city lights, my breath catching in my throat. A strange, hollow sensation settled deep in my chest, like a sudden vacuum where excitement used to reside.

[story 3]

I picked up my phone again, my fingers clumsy as I navigated back to the thread. The cancellation notice sat there, an unmovable block of text. For a full minute, I just held the device, my knuckles turning white where I gripped it. Then, a small, involuntary sound escaped me—a sharp, breathy little laugh that quickly dissolved into a choked gasp. I dropped the phone onto the desk; it made a dull thud that seemed to echo the sudden emptiness inside me. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between the sofa and the kitchen counter. My heart was hammering against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the sudden, overwhelming urge to just walk away from everything, to dissolve into the very air around me.
